answers.intake_q1: Can you describe the chest pain? For example, is it sharp, dull, pressure-like, or burning?
answers.intake_q2: When did the chest pain start?
answers.intake_q3: Where exactly is the chest pain located, and does it radiate anywhere?
answers.intake_a3: next to sternum on the right
answers.intake_q4: Any shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, or dizziness with the chest pain?
answers.intake_q5: Have you noticed any swelling, redness, or tenderness in your chest or nearby areas?
answers.intake_q6: Have you had any recent injuries, heavy lifting, or strenuous activity that might have caused muscle strain?
answers.intake_q7: Any cough, fever, or recent respiratory infections?
answers.intake_q8: Any history of heart disease, blood clots, or lung problems?
answers.intake_summary: A patient presents with sharp, right parasternal chest pain for 1 week, tender to palpation, following heavy lifting. No associated shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, dizziness, cough, fever, or history of cardiac or pulmonary disease. Findings suggest musculoskeletal chest wall pain.

note.soap:
S: The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with sharp chest pain located next to the right side of the sternum, ongoing for one week. The pain is tender to palpation and began after heavy lifting. She denies any associated symptoms such as shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, dizziness, cough, or fever. There is no history of heart disease, blood clots, or lung problems. The clinical picture is consistent with musculoskeletal chest wall pain likely related to strain.

O: Phone evaluation only; no vital signs or physical exam available.

A: The working diagnosis is musculoskeletal chest wall pain, likely costochondritis or muscle strain related to heavy lifting. 
Differential: 
1. Costochondritis 
2. Muscle strain of chest wall 
3. Cardiac causes (less likely given absence of risk factors and symptoms) 
4. Pulmonary causes (less likely given no respiratory symptoms). Patient is not pregnant.

P: 
 - First-line medication: Ibuprofen 400 mg orally every 6 to 8 hours as needed for pain, for 7 days. Avoid if history of gastrointestinal ulcer or allergy to NSAIDs. 
 - Alternatives: 
 - Acetaminophen 500 mg orally every 6 hours as needed for pain, for 7 days; avoid if liver disease or allergy. 
 - Naproxen 250 mg orally twice daily as needed for pain, for 7 days; avoid if gastrointestinal ulcer or allergy to NSAIDs. 
 - Medication safety considerations: No known medication allergies, no pregnancy, no renal or liver disease. NSAIDs should be used cautiously if any gastrointestinal issues develop. 
 - Return precautions: Seek urgent care if chest pain worsens, becomes pressure-like or radiates, or if new symptoms such as shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, or dizziness develop. Also return if pain does not improve or worsens over the next 48–72 hours. 
 - Testing: If no improvement within 48–72 hours, worsening symptoms, or recurrent symptoms: seek in-person evaluation for appropriate testing/workup as indicated.

evidence.snippet:

Pain in the neck, jaw, throat, upper belly, or back 
Nausea or vomiting 
Unusual fatigue 
Shortness of breath (feeling like you can't get enough air) during physical activity 
Women who have coronary artery disease are more likely than men to have chest pain when resting or doing daily activities, rather than during exercise. They're also more likely than men to feel chest pain from mental stress.
Symptoms of a heart attack in women may also be different than in men. Chest pain is the most common symptom for both sexes. It may feel like crushing or squeezing. But women are somewhat less likely than men to have chest pa

evidence.title: Turner syndrome - NHS
evidence.snippet:
e typical features or symptoms of the condition are noticed – a blood test can confirm the diagnosis during pregnancy if problems with the heart or kidneys are found during routine ultrasound scans If you're pregnant and your maternity team think your baby could have Turner syndrome, you may be offered tests to check for the condition. This could be either:
chorionic villus sampling (CVS) amniocentesis 
Causes of Turner syndrome 
Turner syndrome is a genetic condition, caused by a missing or partially missing sex chromosome.
It's not possible to prevent Turner syndrome.
Very rarely it can be passed to a child by a parent, but most of the time it happens by chance. It is not linked to age or anything the parents did before or during pregnancy.
How Turner syndrome affects your life 
Turner syndrome can slightly reduce how long someone is expected to live, but most people should be able to

Summary 
Having a pain in your chest can be scary. It does not always mean that you are having a heart attack . There can be many other causes, including:
Other heart problems, such as angina 
Panic attacks 
Digestive problems,

Conditions A to Z
Costochondritis 
Costochondritis is inflammation where your ribs join the bone in the middle of your chest (breastbone). It can cause sharp chest pain, especially when moving or breathing. It usually gets better on its own over time. 
Symptoms of costochondritis 
Costochondritis may cause sharp pain in the front or side of your chest.
The pain may be worse when:
moving your upper body lying down breathing deeply you press the middle of your chest 
Urgent advice: Get help from NHS 111 if:
you think you have costochondritis It is important to get chest pain checked to rule out other more serious conditions.
You can call 111 or get help from 111 online

evidence.title: Congenital heart disease - NHS
evidence.snippet:
face.
These are signs of a serious medical problem.
Information: 
Do not drive yourself to A&E. 
The person you speak to at 999 will give you advice about what to do.
Urgent advice: Ask for an urgent GP appointment or get help from NHS 111 if:
you think you or your child may have symptoms of congenital heart disease You can call 111 or get help from 111 online . Call 111 if you need advice for a child under 5 years old.
Diagnosing congenital heart disease 
Congenital heart disease is screened for during the 20-week pregnancy screening scan . The 20-week scan cannot find all conditions, so it’s possible for congenital heart disease to be misse
